healthMetrics.cigMethodologyNote: Berkeley Earth conversion: 22.0 µg/m³ PM2.5 ≈ 1 cigarette/day. This compares population-level mortality risk, not individual clinical outcomes. Acute vs chronic exposure differs significantly.
healthMetrics.aqliMethodologyNote: AQLI methodology: each 10 µg/m³ PM2.5 above WHO baseline (5.0 µg/m³) ≈ 0.98 years of life expectancy lost. Calculated from annual mean PM2.5 (long-term exposure), not annual median.

seasonalEvents.event: Worst burning season in SE Asia
seasonalEvents.cause: Upland deforestation burning + crop residue burning across the Mekong sub-region
Heavy rainfall clears smoke and dust
Mountain topography traps cool-season pollution in the valley

methodology.cigaretteEquivalence: Berkeley Earth conversion: 22.0 µg/m³ PM2.5 ≈ 1 cigarette/day. This compares population-level mortality risk, not individual clinical outcomes. Acute vs chronic exposure differs significantly.
methodology.aqli: AQLI methodology: each 10 µg/m³ PM2.5 above WHO baseline (5.0 µg/m³) ≈ 0.98 years of life expectancy lost. Calculated from annual mean PM2.5 (long-term exposure), not annual median.
methodology.dataSource: OpenAQ API v3 /sensors/{id}/days pre-computed daily aggregates

section3.title: The Burning Season
section3.narrative: Every year between February and April, Chiang Mai transforms. The mountain bowl geography that makes the city scenic—flanked by Doi Suthep to the west and highland ranges on all sides—becomes a natural smoke trap. Farmers across the wider Mekong sub-region burn fields: deforestation clearing, crop residue, slash-and-burn agriculture. The smoke drifts in and stays. March median: 54 µg/m³. Hazardous readings occurred on 16.5% of all March days in our dataset—roughly one in six days too dangerous for outdoor activity even for healthy adults. April is nearly as bad at 53 µg/m³.

section5.narrative: No city in our dataset shows a starker seasonal swing. September median: 6 µg/m³—among the cleanest urban air in Asia. March median: 54 µg/m³—hazardous by any standard. That is an 8.9× difference in the same city across the same year. The burning season is not a bad few days; it is a two-month annual crisis that recurs with near-certainty. The monsoon is not just relief—it is the only force powerful enough to clear the smoke from the valley. Plan your visit around it.

section6.title: Your Breathing Window
section6.intro: Chiang Mai's air quality is among the most polarised of any city we track. The monsoon months are genuinely exceptional. The burning season is genuinely dangerous. The window between them is narrow but predictable.

bestWindow.detail: Southwest monsoon brings heavy rainfall that scrubs the air clean. July and September both average around 7 µg/m³—WHO Good territory. Jogging, cycling, and all outdoor activity are safe on virtually every day. This is the ideal time to visit.

caution.detail: Post-monsoon transition and the early cool season. October is usually fine at 11 µg/m³. November begins to deteriorate as early burning starts. December and January bring valley inversion haze—not as severe as burning season, but masks are wise on hazy days.

highRisk.detail: Burning season. Avoid if you have respiratory conditions. If you must be in Chiang Mai, stay indoors during peak burn days, run air purifiers, and check hourly readings before any outdoor activity. March is the worst month: 54 µg/m³ median with hazardous days in every year of our dataset.
